name: Sirius
desc: Brightest star in the night sky. A white main-sequence star with a faint white dwarf companion.

name: Canopus
desc: Second brightest star. A yellow-white supergiant used for spacecraft navigation.

name: Arcturus
desc: Brightest star in the northern hemisphere. An orange giant nearing the end of its life.

name: Vega
desc: Once the North Star 12,000 years ago. Will be again in 13,727 AD.

name: Capella
desc: A quadruple star system. Two giant yellow stars orbiting each other closely.

name: Rigel
desc: Blue supergiant 120,000× more luminous than the Sun. Orion's left foot.

name: Procyon
desc: One of our closest stellar neighbors. Has a white dwarf companion.

name: Betelgeuse
desc: Red supergiant that could explode as a supernova any day now. Orion's right shoulder.

name: Achernar
desc: One of the flattest stars known — spins so fast it bulges at its equator.

name: Hadar
desc: A triple star system. One of the pointer stars to the Southern Cross.

name: Altair
desc: Rotates in just 10 hours — so fast it's visibly flattened. Part of the Summer Triangle.

name: Acrux
desc: Brightest star in the Southern Cross. Appears on Australia's flag.

name: Aldebaran
desc: The fiery eye of the Bull. An orange giant 44× bigger than the Sun.

name: Antares
desc: The rival of Mars — named for its red color. A red supergiant 700× the Sun's diameter.

name: Spica
desc: A close binary system where the stars nearly touch. Over 12,000× our Sun's luminosity.

name: Pollux
desc: The brighter twin. Has a confirmed exoplanet — Pollux b.

name: Fomalhaut
desc: Surrounded by a massive dust ring. One of the first stars with a directly imaged exoplanet.

name: Deneb
desc: If placed where Sirius is, it would cast shadows on Earth. Insanely luminous.

name: Mimosa
desc: Second star of the Southern Cross. A Beta Cephei variable that pulsates.

name: Regulus
desc: Heart of the Lion. Spins so fast that it's shaped like a pumpkin.

name: Castor
desc: Looks like one star but is actually SIX stars orbiting each other.

name: Bellatrix
desc: The Amazon Star — Orion's left shoulder. A blue giant that will become an orange giant.

name: Alnilam
desc: The middle star of Orion's Belt. One of the most luminous stars visible to the naked eye.

name: Alnitak
desc: Left star of Orion's Belt. Near the famous Horsehead Nebula.

name: Mintaka
desc: Right star of Orion's Belt. Almost perfectly on the celestial equator.

name: Dubhe
desc: The upper lip of the Big Dipper's cup. A pointer to Polaris.

name: Merak
desc: The bottom pointer star. Draw a line through Merak and Dubhe to find Polaris.

name: Polaris
desc: The current North Star. A Cepheid variable — it actually pulses in brightness.

name: Mizar
desc: The middle star of the Big Dipper's handle. A famous naked-eye double star with Alcor.

name: Saiph
desc: Orion's right knee. Almost as far and luminous as Rigel, but appears dimmer.

name: Alpheratz
desc: Shared between Andromeda and Pegasus. A mercury-manganese star with odd chemistry.

name: Mirach
desc: A red giant used as a stepping stone to find the Andromeda Galaxy.

name: Algol
desc: The Demon Star — it blinks every 2.87 days as its companion eclipses it.

name: Thuban
desc: Was the North Star when the Great Pyramid was built, around 2700 BC.

name: Schedar
desc: The brightest star in the W-shaped Cassiopeia.

name: Caph
desc: The leading star of Cassiopeia's W. A Delta Scuti variable.

name: Denebola
desc: The Lion's tail. Surrounded by a debris disk — possible planet formation.

name: Alioth
desc: The brightest star in the Big Dipper. An Alpha-2 Canum Venaticorum variable.

name: Alkaid
desc: End of the Big Dipper's handle. NOT part of the Ursa Major moving group.

name: Kochab
desc: Was the pole star 3000 years ago. An orange giant with a confirmed exoplanet.

name: Wezen
desc: One of the most luminous stars visible — 82,000× the Sun's luminosity.

name: Sargas
desc: The tail of the Scorpion. An evolved giant star.

name: Diphda
desc: Brightest star in Cetus. An orange giant about 145× the Sun's luminosity.

name: Menkalinan
desc: An eclipsing binary system. Two identical white stars orbiting each other.

name: Alhena
desc: The foot of the Pollux twin. A brilliant white subgiant.

name: Peacock
desc: The eye of the Peacock constellation. A hot blue-white subgiant star.

name: Alphard
desc: The Solitary One — the only bright star in the largest constellation.

name: Hamal
desc: Once marked the vernal equinox 2000 years ago. Still defines the First Point of Aries.

name: Nunki
desc: One of the oldest named stars. Points toward the galactic center.

name: Rasalhague
desc: Head of the Serpent-Bearer. The Sun passes through Ophiuchus every December.

name: Sun
desc: Our home star. A G-type main-sequence star, 4.6 billion years old. Contains 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

name: Moon
desc: Earth's only natural satellite. Controls our tides and has been walked on by 12 humans.

name: Mercury
desc: Smallest planet. Surface temperatures swing from -180°C to 430°C. A year lasts just 88 Earth days.

name: Venus
desc: Brightest planet in our sky. Rotates backwards. Surface hot enough to melt lead (465°C).

name: Mars
desc: The Red Planet. Home to Olympus Mons — the tallest volcano in the solar system at 21km.

name: Jupiter
desc: King of planets. The Great Red Spot is a storm larger than Earth that has raged for 350+ years.

name: Saturn
desc: The ringed wonder. Its rings span 282,000 km but are only 10 meters thick. Could float in a bathtub.

name: Uranus
desc: The ice giant that rolls on its side. Has 27 known moons named after Shakespeare characters.

name: Neptune
desc: Windiest planet — 2,100 km/h winds. Was predicted mathematically before it was ever seen.
